groups.title: Essentials
groups.description: Provider, client, offer, proposal context, client needs, decision audience, goal, next step, language, and length.

groups.title: Client Strategy
groups.description: Relationship stage, market context, decision criteria, stakeholders, positioning, alternatives, and objections.

groups.title: Scope & Delivery
groups.description: Deliverables, method, phases, timeline, start date, client responsibilities, assumptions, and exclusions.

groups.title: Pricing & Commercial Terms
groups.description: Optional pricing model, prices, currency, payment terms, add-ons, validity, and commercial notes.

groups.title: Evidence, Brand & Output
groups.description: Differentiators, proof, source material, brand voice, tone, proposal features, and final requirements.

variables.label: Provider or company name
variables.placeholder: Acme Consulting or Jane Smith
variables.hint: The business, agency, consultant, or provider submitting the proposal.

help.what: Provide the provider or company name for this sales proposal.
help.why: The proposal must clearly identify the party offering the solution.
help.example: Good example: Acme Consulting
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, unsupported client knowledge, guaranteed outcomes, or facts not approved for the proposal.

variables.label: Client or organization
variables.placeholder: Northstar Retail Group
variables.hint: The company, team, or individual receiving the proposal.

help.what: Provide the client or organization for this sales proposal.
help.why: A sales proposal must be tailored to a specific recipient rather than written generically.
help.example: Good example: Northstar Retail Group
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, unsupported client knowledge, guaranteed outcomes, or facts not approved for the proposal.

variables.label: Solution or offer name
variables.placeholder: Customer Support Automation Program
variables.hint: Name the service, project, product, package, or solution being proposed.

help.what: Provide the solution or offer name for this sales proposal.
help.why: The offer name anchors the title, solution description, and scope.
help.example: Good example: Customer Support Automation Program
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, unsupported client knowledge, guaranteed outcomes, or facts not approved for the proposal.

variables.label: Offer overview
variables.placeholder: Explain what you are proposing, how it works, and what it includes at a high level.
variables.hint: Describe the proposed solution without relying on unsupported marketing claims.

help.what: Provide the offer overview for this sales proposal.
help.why: The model cannot accurately define the proposed solution without the provider’s actual offer details.
help.example: Good example: A six-week implementation that maps support workflows, configures automation, trains the team, and hands over reporting.
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, unsupported client knowledge, guaranteed outcomes, or facts not approved for the proposal.

variables.label: Client’s current situation
variables.placeholder: Describe the client’s current process, environment, opportunity, or relevant background.
variables.hint: Include only known information and avoid assumptions about the client.

help.what: Provide the client’s current situation for this sales proposal.
help.why: The proposal must demonstrate an accurate understanding of the client’s starting point.
help.example: Good example: The client handles support through shared inboxes and wants a clearer escalation process before seasonal demand increases.
help.avoid: Avoid inventing client problems, prior conversations, approvals, or internal priorities that were not supplied.

variables.label: Challenges to address
variables.placeholder: List the main problems, constraints, inefficiencies, or risks the proposal should address.
variables.hint: Combine related problems instead of listing every minor symptom separately.

help.what: Provide the challenges to address for this sales proposal.
help.why: The proposal’s argument and scope must connect directly to the client’s actual challenges.
help.example: Good example: Slow response times, inconsistent routing, limited reporting, and manual status updates.
help.avoid: Avoid inventing client problems, prior conversations, approvals, or internal priorities that were not supplied.

variables.label: Desired outcomes
variables.placeholder: What should improve, change, or become possible if the project succeeds?
variables.hint: Use realistic goals; include measurable targets only when they are approved.

help.what: Provide the desired outcomes for this sales proposal.
help.why: The proposal must explain the value and intended destination of the engagement.
help.example: Good example: Reduce manual triage, improve visibility into response times, and create a repeatable support workflow.
help.avoid: Avoid guaranteed results, fake ROI, unsupported metrics, or outcomes outside the provider’s control.

variables.label: Decision audience
variables.placeholder: CEO, operations director, procurement team, technical reviewers, or other readers.
variables.hint: Describe the people who will review, influence, approve, or use the proposal.

help.what: Provide the decision audience for this sales proposal.
help.why: Content depth, terminology, evidence, and commercial emphasis depend on the buying audience.
help.example: Good example: COO, head of support, procurement manager, and technical reviewer.
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, unsupported client knowledge, guaranteed outcomes, or facts not approved for the proposal.

variables.label: Proposal goal
variables.placeholder: Select the main goal
variables.hint: Choose the primary commercial outcome the proposal should support.

help.what: Optional context for the proposal goal field.
help.why: Different proposal goals require different emphasis, next steps, and levels of detail.
help.example: Good example: Win approval
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, hidden commercial terms, unsupported claims, private data, or manipulative pressure.

variables.label: Preferred next step
variables.placeholder: Schedule a review call, approve the scope, sign, or start a pilot.
variables.hint: Leave as Auto for the model to choose one appropriate low-friction action.

help.what: Optional context for the preferred next step field.
help.why: A proposal needs one clear and commercially appropriate action for the client.
help.example: Good example: Schedule a proposal review call
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, hidden commercial terms, unsupported claims, private data, or manipulative pressure.

variables.label: Output language
variables.placeholder: English
variables.hint: Enter the language in which the complete proposal should be written.

help.what: Provide the output language for this sales proposal.
help.why: The language cannot always be inferred from company names or source notes.
help.example: Good example: English
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, unsupported client knowledge, guaranteed outcomes, or facts not approved for the proposal.

variables.label: Proposal length
variables.placeholder: Select the desired depth
variables.hint: Standard suits most proposals; Auto chooses the shortest useful length.

help.what: Optional context for the proposal length field.
help.why: The appropriate depth varies between a simple service offer and a complex multi-phase engagement.
help.example: Good example: Standard
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, hidden commercial terms, unsupported claims, private data, or manipulative pressure.

variables.label: Sales relationship stage
variables.placeholder: Select the current stage
variables.hint: Prevents the proposal from implying conversations or agreements that did not occur.

help.what: Optional context for the sales relationship stage field.
help.why: A new-prospect proposal should read differently from an RFP, renewal, or post-discovery proposal.
help.example: Good example: After discovery
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, hidden commercial terms, unsupported claims, private data, or manipulative pressure.

variables.label: Industry or market context
variables.placeholder: Relevant market conditions, regulations, trends, or operational realities.
variables.hint: Add only context that is known and relevant to the decision.

help.what: Optional context for the industry or market context field.
help.why: Relevant market context can materially affect urgency, positioning, terminology, and risk.
help.example: Good example: Retail support volume increases during Q4 and requires faster escalation handling.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, hidden commercial terms, unsupported claims, private data, or manipulative pressure.

variables.label: Decision criteria
variables.placeholder: Budget, implementation speed, security, expertise, support, scalability, or other criteria.
variables.hint: List the factors the client is likely to use when evaluating the proposal.

help.what: Optional context for the decision criteria field.
help.why: The proposal should make it easy for decision-makers to evaluate the offer against their real criteria.
help.example: Good example: Implementation speed, low disruption, reporting visibility, support quality, and total cost.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, hidden commercial terms, unsupported claims, private data, or manipulative pressure.

variables.label: Stakeholders and roles
variables.placeholder: Who approves, evaluates, implements, uses, or funds the proposed work?
variables.hint: Include names only when they are appropriate for the client-facing proposal.

help.what: Optional context for the stakeholders and roles field.
help.why: Different stakeholders may require different evidence, explanations, and implementation details.
help.example: Good example: Head of support owns workflow approval; IT reviews integrations; procurement reviews terms.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, hidden commercial terms, unsupported claims, private data, or manipulative pressure.

variables.label: Primary positioning angle
variables.placeholder: Select the main value angle
variables.hint: Choose the value dimension that should receive the strongest emphasis.

help.what: Optional context for the primary positioning angle field.
help.why: A proposal becomes more persuasive when it prioritizes the value dimension most relevant to the client.
help.example: Good example: Risk reduction
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, hidden commercial terms, unsupported claims, private data, or manipulative pressure.

variables.label: Alternatives being considered
variables.placeholder: Internal delivery, another provider, delaying the project, or a named alternative.
variables.hint: Include only known alternatives; the proposal will avoid unsupported competitor claims.

help.what: Optional context for the alternatives being considered field.
help.why: The proposal may need to explain relevant trade-offs without attacking competitors.
help.example: Good example: Internal workflow cleanup and one other automation provider.
help.avoid: Avoid unsupported competitor criticism, fake comparisons, or claims that alternatives cannot work.

variables.label: Likely objections or concerns
variables.placeholder: Budget concerns, implementation risk, timing, adoption, security, or internal capacity.
variables.hint: List concerns the proposal should address calmly and factually.

help.what: Optional context for the likely objections or concerns field.
help.why: Known objections materially affect scope explanation, evidence, risk handling, and next steps.
help.example: Good example: Concern about team adoption, migration effort, and unclear ownership after launch.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, hidden commercial terms, unsupported claims, private data, or manipulative pressure.

variables.label: Deliverables
variables.placeholder: List the reports, designs, services, systems, sessions, assets, or other outputs.
variables.hint: Include quantities and formats only when they are confirmed.

help.what: Optional context for the deliverables field.
help.why: Clear deliverables reduce uncertainty and define what the client will receive.
help.example: Good example: Workflow map, automation configuration, team training, reporting dashboard, and handover guide.
help.avoid: Avoid invented quantities, service levels, revision rounds, acceptance criteria, or delivery methods.

variables.label: Approach and method
variables.placeholder: Explain how the work will be performed, managed, reviewed, or validated.
variables.hint: Focus on the process details that help the client evaluate delivery confidence.

help.what: Optional context for the approach and method field.
help.why: The delivery method often differentiates otherwise similar offers.
help.example: Good example: Discovery, configuration, review cycles, pilot testing, training, and handover.
help.avoid: Avoid invented quantities, service levels, revision rounds, acceptance criteria, or delivery methods.

variables.label: Phases and milestones
variables.placeholder: Discovery, planning, implementation, testing, launch, handover, or review stages.
variables.hint: Include milestone names, outputs, or approval points when known.

help.what: Optional context for the phases and milestones field.
help.why: Complex proposals are easier to approve when the work is divided into understandable stages.
help.example: Good example: Week 1 discovery, weeks 2-4 configuration, week 5 pilot, week 6 training and handover.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, hidden commercial terms, unsupported claims, private data, or manipulative pressure.

variables.label: Timeline or duration
variables.placeholder: Eight weeks, phased over Q4, or timing dependent on client approvals.
variables.hint: Use ranges or dependencies when exact dates are not confirmed.

help.what: Optional context for the timeline or duration field.
help.why: Timing is a central evaluation factor in most sales proposals.
help.example: Good example: Six weeks after contract signature and access to required systems.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, hidden commercial terms, unsupported claims, private data, or manipulative pressure.

variables.label: Proposed start date
variables.placeholder: Select a date
variables.hint: Leave blank when the start depends on approval, contracting, or availability.

help.what: Optional context for the proposed start date field.
help.why: A confirmed start date can clarify scheduling without forcing the model to invent one.
help.example: Good example: 2026-09-14
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, hidden commercial terms, unsupported claims, private data, or manipulative pressure.

variables.label: Client responsibilities
variables.placeholder: Access, approvals, source files, data, feedback, staff availability, or decisions.
variables.hint: Specify what the client must provide for successful delivery.

help.what: Optional context for the client responsibilities field.
help.why: Client dependencies can affect schedule, cost, and successful implementation.
help.example: Good example: Provide system access, approve workflows, attend review sessions, and nominate a support owner.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, hidden commercial terms, unsupported claims, private data, or manipulative pressure.

variables.label: Project assumptions
variables.placeholder: State conditions on which the scope, timing, or pricing is based.
variables.hint: Use assumptions to clarify the offer, not to hide important limitations.

help.what: Optional context for the project assumptions field.
help.why: Commercial and delivery commitments may depend on specific operating assumptions.
help.example: Good example: Client systems remain available and integration scope stays within the approved support tools.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, hidden commercial terms, unsupported claims, private data, or manipulative pressure.

variables.label: Out-of-scope items
variables.placeholder: List work, expenses, licenses, support, or services not included.
variables.hint: Include only exclusions that are important for avoiding scope ambiguity.

help.what: Optional context for the out-of-scope items field.
help.why: Explicit exclusions prevent the proposal from unintentionally promising work that is not included.
help.example: Good example: Custom software development, 24/7 managed support, and third-party license fees.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, hidden commercial terms, unsupported claims, private data, or manipulative pressure.

variables.label: Include pricing
variables.placeholder: Not applicable
variables.hint: Enable this only when approved pricing or commercial terms should appear.

help.what: Optional context for the include pricing field.
help.why: Some proposals include full commercial terms, while others intentionally focus on scope and value.
help.example: Good example: On
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, hidden commercial terms, unsupported claims, private data, or manipulative pressure.

variables.label: Pricing model
variables.placeholder: Select the pricing structure
variables.hint: Choose the commercial model used for the proposed work.

help.what: Optional context for the pricing model field.
help.why: The pricing structure determines how commercial information should be organized and explained.
help.example: Good example: Fixed fee
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, hidden commercial terms, unsupported claims, private data, or manipulative pressure.

variables.label: Prices and fees
variables.placeholder: List approved prices, rates, package amounts, billing periods, or fee ranges.
variables.hint: Include exact figures only; the model will not invent or estimate missing prices.

help.what: Optional context for the prices and fees field.
help.why: Pricing is factual commercial information that must come directly from the provider.
help.example: Good example: Implementation package: $12,000 fixed fee.
help.avoid: Avoid estimated prices, hidden fees, invented discounts, missing currency, or unclear tax treatment.

variables.label: Currency and tax treatment
variables.placeholder: EUR excluding VAT, USD including applicable taxes, or similar wording.
variables.hint: Clarify currency and whether taxes are included, excluded, or handled separately.

help.what: Optional context for the currency and tax treatment field.
help.why: Prices can be misleading or commercially incomplete without currency and tax treatment.
help.example: Good example: USD excluding applicable taxes
help.avoid: Avoid estimated prices, hidden fees, invented discounts, missing currency, or unclear tax treatment.

variables.label: Payment terms
variables.placeholder: Deposit, milestone billing, monthly invoicing, due dates, or payment schedule.
variables.hint: Enter only approved payment conditions.

help.what: Optional context for the payment terms field.
help.why: Payment timing and structure can materially affect approval and contract expectations.
help.example: Good example: 50% deposit, 50% on handover.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, hidden commercial terms, unsupported claims, private data, or manipulative pressure.

variables.label: Optional add-ons
variables.placeholder: List optional upgrades, extra services, support plans, or additional packages.
variables.hint: Separate optional items clearly from the required proposal price.

help.what: Optional context for the optional add-ons field.
help.why: Optional items require clear separation so the client can distinguish base scope from upgrades.
help.example: Good example: Monthly reporting review, additional training sessions, and extended support.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, hidden commercial terms, unsupported claims, private data, or manipulative pressure.

variables.label: Proposal valid until
variables.placeholder: Select a date
variables.hint: Use only when the pricing or commercial terms have a genuine validity period.

help.what: Optional context for the proposal valid until field.
help.why: A real validity date may be commercially important, but must never be invented for urgency.
help.example: Good example: 2026-10-01
help.avoid: Avoid artificial scarcity, fake urgency, or arbitrary validity dates used only to pressure the client.

variables.label: Other commercial terms
variables.placeholder: Expenses, travel, licensing, renewal, cancellation, or procurement notes.
variables.hint: Add important commercial conditions not covered by pricing or payment fields.

help.what: Optional context for the other commercial terms field.
help.why: Some offers contain material commercial conditions that do not fit the main pricing structure.
help.example: Good example: Third-party software licenses are billed directly by the vendor.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, hidden commercial terms, unsupported claims, private data, or manipulative pressure.

variables.label: Relevant differentiators
variables.placeholder: Explain what makes the offer, team, process, or experience especially suitable.
variables.hint: Use specific, supportable differences rather than generic superiority claims.

help.what: Optional context for the relevant differentiators field.
help.why: Differentiators help explain why the proposed provider is a suitable choice.
help.example: Good example: Prior experience implementing support workflows for retail operations teams.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, hidden commercial terms, unsupported claims, private data, or manipulative pressure.

variables.label: Approved proof and examples
variables.placeholder: Approved results, case examples, testimonials, credentials, or relevant experience.
variables.hint: Include only material that may be quoted or summarized in the proposal.

help.what: Optional context for the approved proof and examples field.
help.why: Credibility evidence must be supplied and approved rather than fabricated by the model.
help.example: Good example: Approved case example: reduced manual routing steps for a regional retailer.
help.avoid: Avoid fabricated testimonials, case studies, client names, metrics, ratings, awards, or credentials.

variables.label: References or source material
variables.placeholder: Paste source facts, links, internal notes, RFP requirements, or approved supporting material.
variables.hint: The model will use this material without claiming independent verification.

help.what: Optional context for the references or source material field.
help.why: Source material improves factual accuracy and helps satisfy formal proposal requirements.
help.example: Good example: Discovery notes, support workflow audit, and approved pricing sheet.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, hidden commercial terms, unsupported claims, private data, or manipulative pressure.

variables.label: Brand voice
variables.placeholder: Professional and practical, premium and concise, friendly and collaborative, or similar.
variables.hint: Describe the established voice rather than general writing-quality rules.

help.what: Optional context for the brand voice field.
help.why: The proposal should sound consistent with the provider’s existing communication style.
help.example: Good example: Professional, practical, collaborative, and concise.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, hidden commercial terms, unsupported claims, private data, or manipulative pressure.

variables.label: Proposal tone
variables.placeholder: Select the tone
variables.hint: Choose the overall communication style appropriate for the client and decision.

help.what: Optional context for the proposal tone field.
help.why: Tone materially affects how the same commercial proposal is perceived by different audiences.
help.example: Good example: Clear and consultative
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, hidden commercial terms, unsupported claims, private data, or manipulative pressure.

variables.label: Optional proposal features
variables.placeholder: Select any additional components
variables.hint: Choose only the structural components useful for this proposal.

help.what: Optional context for the optional proposal features field.
help.why: Proposal components should reflect the complexity and delivery context rather than follow one fixed document structure.
help.example: Good example: Executive snapshot, Implementation table, Risk table, Acceptance block, Email cover note
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, hidden commercial terms, unsupported claims, private data, or manipulative pressure.

variables.label: Additional requirements
variables.placeholder: Add required sections, wording restrictions, confidentiality notes, or other instructions.
variables.hint: Use this for important requirements not already covered by another field.

help.what: Optional context for the additional requirements field.
help.why: A single controlled field accommodates genuine exceptions without creating several miscellaneous inputs.
help.example: Good example: Avoid guaranteed ROI claims and mark any missing integration details for review.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, hidden commercial terms, unsupported claims, private data, or manipulative pressure.
